pubmed_links: [Pitts et al. (2015) Brain-testes selenium competition and sex differences in neurodevelopmental disease risk](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26586820/);[Byrns et al. (2014) Mice lacking selenoprotein P and Scly show neurodegeneration and audiogenic seizures](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24519931/);[Kremer et al. (2019) Disruption of selenium handling during puberty causes sex-specific neurological impairments](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31022880/)
summary: SCLY encodes selenocysteine lyase, which recycles selenium from selenocysteine for selenoprotein synthesis; Scly knockout mouse models exhibit neurodegeneration, audiogenic seizures, and reduced parvalbumin interneuron density—a hallmark of schizophrenia—with sex-specific effects linked to aberrant excitatory/inhibitory balance relevant to neurodevelopmental conditions. Evidence is currently confined to animal models with mechanistic implications for schizophrenia and epilepsy rather than direct human genetic association. (emerging evidence)
